C:
Lead with the punchline: good installs but bad day-7 almost never means "day 7 is broken." It means new users aren't reaching first value, so they never form a reason to come back. Day-7 is a lagging symptom; the disease is in the first session and first day. So the work is diagnosis first, and the first fix is almost certainly your first-run experience — not a day-7 re-engagement campaign.

Here's how I'd approach it.

**1. Read the curve before touching anything.** Pull retention as a cohort curve (D0→D1→D3→D7→D30), not a single D7 number, and segment it — by acquisition source, platform, and first-session behavior. The *shape* names the problem:
- Craters by D1–D3 then flattens near zero → activation/onboarding failure (users never "got it").
- Decent D1 but a steady slope with no flattening → no habit, nothing pulls them back.
- A "smile" (drops, then a segment recovers) → some cohort found value; find out who and why, then make more users look like them.

Segmenting also catches the cheap wins: if one ad source has great installs and terrible retention, you're buying the wrong users and no product change will fix that.

**2. Define the activation moment.** Find the early action that separates retained users from churned ones — e.g. "completed first task," "saw 3 pieces of content," "connected an account," "invited someone." Then measure what % of new users reach it in their first session. That completion rate is your real leading metric; D7 follows it.

**3. Fix time-to-first-value.** This is where the biggest drop almost always is, and it compounds into every later cohort. Cut steps before the aha moment, defer signup/permission walls until after the user has felt value, and make the empty first state do work (sample data, a guided first action). Watch session recordings or actually talk to ~10 churned users — qualitative beats dashboards for the "why."

**4. Then build a reason to return.** Only after activation works: lifecycle triggers tied to real value (not generic "we miss you" spam), fresh content or state changes worth coming back for, and a habit hook. Earned notifications, sent because something relevant happened, not on a schedule.

**5. Iterate one lever at a time.** Pick the single biggest drop-off, ship one change, measure the same cohort curve. Retention work is a sequence of targeted bets, not a redesign.

Where to focus first, concretely: spend the first chunk purely on **diagnosis + the activation rate** (steps 1–2). Don't build features yet — instrument the install→open→activation→D1→D7 funnel and find the cliff. Then put the first real engineering effort into **first-session activation** (step 3), because that's both the largest leak and the one whose fix lifts every future cohort. Re-engagement (step 4) is second, and it only pays off once users have actually reached value once.

If you can share the actual curve shape or your current funnel numbers, I can tell you which of these is your bottleneck instead of guessing — that's the one input I can't derive myself.

Lead with the punchline: good installs but bad day-7 almost never means "day 7 is broken." It means new users aren't reaching first value, so they never form a reason to come back. Day-7 is a lagging symptom; the disease is in the first session and first day. So the work is diagnosis first, and the first fix is almost certainly your first-run experience — not a day-7 re-engagement campaign.

**The goal, stated concretely:** move **day-7 retention up by 30–50% relative** (e.g. from 10% to 13–15%) **within one quarter (about 12 weeks)**. The leading metric we'll actually steer by week to week is **first-session activation rate** — the % of new users who hit the activation moment defined in step 2 — because D7 lags it. Target there: lift activation by a similar relative margin first, since the D7 gain follows it by a cohort or two.

Here's how I'd approach it.

Segmenting also catches the cheap wins: if one ad source has great installs and terrible retention, you're buying the wrong users and no product change will fix that.

Then measure what % of new users reach it in their first session. That completion rate is your real leading metric; D7 follows it.

**3. Fix time-to-first-value.** This is where the biggest drop almost always is, and it compounds into every later cohort. Cut steps before the aha moment, defer signup/permission walls until after the user has felt value, and make the empty first state do work (sample data, a guided first action). Watch session recordings or actually talk to ~10 churned users — qualitative beats dashboards for the "why."

**4. Then build a reason to return.** Only after activation works: lifecycle triggers tied to real value (not generic "we miss you" spam), fresh content or state changes worth coming back for, and a habit hook. Earned notifications, sent because something relevant happened, not on a schedule.

**5. Iterate one lever at a time.** Pick the single biggest drop-off, ship one change, measure the same cohort curve. Retention work is a sequence of targeted bets, not a redesign.

Where to focus first, concretely: spend the first chunk purely on **diagnosis + the activation rate** (steps 1–2). Don't build features yet — instrument the install→open→activation→D1→D7 funnel and find the cliff. Then put the first real engineering effort into **first-session activation** (step 3), because that's both the largest leak and the one whose fix lifts every future cohort. Re-engagement (step 4) is second, and it only pays off once users have actually reached value once.

**The #1 test to run first:** once diagnosis confirms the cliff is pre-activation (it almost always is), ship a single change to the first-run flow — **defer the signup/permission wall until after the user has hit the activation moment** (paired with a guided first action so the empty state isn't a dead end). Run it as an A/B on new-install cohorts and read first-session activation rate as the primary metric, with D1 and D7 as the confirming downstream reads. This is the one bet to make first because it attacks the largest, earliest leak and lifts every later cohort.

Explicitly **hold for later** (do *not* run these first, even though they're tempting): day-7 push/re-engagement campaigns, lifecycle email/notification sequences, referral or habit-loop features, and any broad onboarding redesign. They only pay off after activation works, and running them now would spend effort downstream of the real leak — so they're the second wave, gated on the activation test moving.

If you can share the actual curve shape or your current funnel numbers, I can tell you which of these is your bottleneck instead of guessing — that's the one input I can't derive myself.
P:
Lead with the punchline: good installs but bad day-7 almost never means "day 7 is broken." It means new users never reach first value, so they never form a reason to come back. Day-7 is a lagging symptom — the disease is in the first session and the first day. So this is a diagnosis problem before it's a build problem, and the first real fix is almost certainly your first-run experience, not a day-7 win-back campaign.

**Where to focus first, in one line:** instrument the funnel and find your activation rate before you build anything — then put your first engineering effort into first-session time-to-value, because that's both the largest leak and the one fix that lifts every future cohort at once.

Here's the full sketch.

**1. Read the curve before touching anything.** Pull retention as a cohort curve (D0→D1→D3→D7→D30), not a single D7 number, and segment it by acquisition source, platform, and first-session behavior. The *shape* names the problem:
- Craters by D1–D3, then flattens near zero → activation failure. Users never understood what the app was for.
- Healthy D1 but a steady downward slope that never flattens → no habit. Nothing earns a return.
- A "smile" — most churn, but one segment climbs back → some cohort found real value. Identify exactly who they are and what they did, then make more new users look like them.

Do the cheapest check here first: if one ad source has great installs and terrible retention, you're buying the wrong users and no product change will fix it. That's a free win and it's free to rule out — do it before you write a line of code.

**2. Define the activation moment.** Find the early action that most separates retained users from churned ones — completed first task, viewed three pieces of content, connected an account, invited someone. Then measure the share of new users who reach it in their first session. That completion rate is your real leading metric; D7 follows it. If you can only instrument one thing this week, instrument this.

**3. Fix time-to-first-value.** This is where the biggest drop almost always sits, and fixing it compounds into every cohort that follows. Concretely: cut steps before the aha moment, defer signup and permission walls until after the user has felt value, and make the empty first state do work — sample data, a guided first action, a populated screen instead of a blank one. Pair the dashboard with qualitative work: watch ten session recordings or talk to ten users who churned. The numbers tell you where the cliff is; only the people tell you why.

**4. Then build a reason to return.** Only after activation works. Lifecycle triggers tied to a real event in the user's own data, not scheduled "we miss you" spam. Fresh content or state changes worth opening the app for. A habit hook that fires because something relevant actually happened. Re-engagement layered on top of a broken first run just pays to re-churn the same users.

**5. Iterate one lever at a time.** Pick the single biggest drop-off, ship one change, re-measure the same cohort curve. Retention is a sequence of targeted bets, not a redesign — and changing two things at once means you learn nothing from either.

The sequencing, then: diagnosis and activation rate first (steps 1–2, no features yet), first-session activation as the first build (step 3), re-engagement second (step 4), and never more than one lever in flight (step 5).

The one input I can't derive myself is your actual curve shape or funnel numbers. Share either and I'll tell you which of these is your real bottleneck instead of playing the odds — though if you want to move before that lands, the odds strongly favor first-session activation.
